content:
6 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Markus Spiske on UnsplashMy production site went down on a Tuesday. Not “slow,” not “a region is degraded” — down. It started with our reverse proxy getting blocked. Vercel’s firewall decided our own traffic looked suspicious and started dropping it. Fine, I thought — I’ll just turn the firewall off.
So I did.
And it kept blocking.
And the worst part wasn’t the outage itself. It was the realization that I had no lever to pull. I opened a support ticket, watched the little “we’ll get back to you” auto-reply land in my inbox, and then just… waited. Refreshed the status page. Waited some more. Refreshed our own site, hoping.
That’s the moment you understand what “convenient” actually costs.
We’d been on Vercel for a long time, and I still think it’s a great product. You push to a branch and it deploys. Preview URLs show up on every pull request. SSL, CDN, rollbacks — all handled. It’s genuinely one of the smoothest developer experiences out there, and for a long stretch that convenience was exactly what we needed.
But convenience and control are two different things. When everything is managed for you, everything is also out of your hands. When the platform has a bad day, you have a bad day, and there is no SSH session you can open, no log you can tail, no service you can restart. You’re a passenger. And on that Tuesday, being a passenger felt a lot like being stuck.
So I started looking for something that would give me back the wheel — without giving up the parts of Vercel I actually liked. That search is how I found Coolify.
What Coolify Actually Is
Coolify is an open-source, self-hosted platform-as-a-service. If you’ve used Vercel, Heroku, or Railway, the mental model is the same: connect a Git repo, and it builds and deploys your app. The difference is where it all runs. Instead of someone else’s platform, it runs on a server you own — a $5 VPS, a beefy dedicated box, an old machine in a closet, even a Raspberry Pi.
The pitch that hooked me: you get autodeploy, preview environments, one-click databases, and automatic SSL — the conveniences — but on infrastructure you control. It’s free (the software is open source; you only pay for the server), and there’s no vendor lock-in, because underneath it’s just Docker containers on a Linux box. If you ever walk away from Coolify, your apps are still just apps on a server you can log into.
That last point mattered to me more than anything after the outage. Whatever went wrong, I wanted to be able to do something about it.
Here’s the Part Nobody Believes: It’s Genuinely Easy
I expected self-hosting to be the usual tax — a weekend lost to config files, reverse proxies, and cryptic Docker errors. It wasn’t. Here’s the whole path, from empty server to deployed app.
Step 1: Get a server
You need any Linux server with SSH access. A VPS from Hetzner, DigitalOcean, or similar is the typical choice. The official minimums are modest — 2 CPU cores, 2 GB of RAM, and 30 GB of disk — and Ubuntu LTS (22.04 or 24.04) is the smoothest path since the install script targets it directly.
If you’re planning to run real production workloads, aim higher. The Coolify team publishes a reference setup: 8 GB RAM, 4 cores, and 150 GB of storage comfortably runs several Node apps, static sites, a couple of Postgres databases, some Redis instances, and a handful of self-hosted tools like analytics and uptime monitoring — all on one box. That’s a lot of stuff for one modest server, and it reframes the cost conversation entirely.
One tip: use a fresh server. Coolify wants to manage the machine, so starting clean avoids conflicts with anything already installed.
Step 2: Run one command
SSH into the server as root and run:
curl -fsSL <https://cdn.coollabs.io/coolify/install.sh|sudobash>That’s the install. The script does the unglamorous work for you: it installs Docker Engine, sets up the directory structure under /data/coolify, generates SSH keys so Coolify can manage the server, and starts the Coolify containers. The whole thing takes about five to ten minutes, most of which is just watching packages download.
Before you run it, make sure ports 80, 443, and 8000 are open on your firewall — 80 and 443 for the apps you’ll deploy, 8000 for the Coolify dashboard itself.
Step 3: Create your admin account
When the script finishes, it prints a URL like http://your-server-ip:8000. Open it in a browser and you'll land on a registration page for your first admin account.
Do this immediately. This is the one security detail worth calling out: the registration page is open until someone claims it, so whoever hits that URL first becomes the owner of the server. Create your account before you do anything else, and you’re safe.
Step 4: Connect a Git repo and deploy
This is where it starts to feel like Vercel again. Inside the dashboard you create a project, then add a resource — an application, a database, a service. Point it at a Git repository (you can connect GitHub via an app for the tightest integration, or just paste a public/private repo URL), pick the branch, and Coolify figures out how to build it.
Push to that branch and it redeploys automatically. That’s the autodeploy behavior I didn’t want to give up, and here it is, running on my own server. Databases are a dropdown — pick Postgres, MySQL, MongoDB, Redis, whatever — and Coolify spins up a container and hands you the connection string. SSL certificates are issued and renewed automatically through Let’s Encrypt the moment you point a domain at your app.
Adding more later is the same loop every time: new resource, connect, deploy. Once the pattern clicks, standing up a new service takes a couple of minutes.
What Changed for Me
The obvious win is money. One VPS can host what used to be several separate platform bills, and the marginal cost of the next side project is basically zero once the server is running. But that wasn’t really the point.
The point was that Tuesday. Now, when something breaks, I can SSH in. I can read the logs. I can restart a container, check disk usage, roll back a deploy, or just see with my own eyes what’s happening. The outage that left me refreshing a status page turned into the reason I no longer have to refresh a status page. If something goes wrong now, it’s mine to fix — and having that option, even when I don’t need it, is the whole difference.
The Honest Caveats
Self-hosting isn’t free in the way that matters most: it costs you responsibility. Vercel’s team keeps the platform patched, scaled, and backed up so you never think about it. On your own server, that’s your job. You own the OS updates, the backups, the monitoring, and the security hardening. Coolify makes the deployment part easy, but it can’t make the server yours and someone else’s problem at the same time.
For a lot of teams, that trade is worth it. For others — especially very small teams with no appetite for any ops work at all — a managed platform is still the right call, and there’s no shame in that. Coolify even offers a hosted “Cloud” version if you want the software without running the server, though at that point you’re back to trusting someone else’s infrastructure.
For me, the balance tipped the day I realized how little I could do when it mattered. Coolify gave me back the ability to act. And the fact that getting there was one curl command and a browser tab still feels a little unreasonable — in the best way.
If you’ve ever sat staring at a status page you had no power to change, it’s worth an afternoon. Spin up a cheap VPS, run the one command, and deploy something. Worst case, you’re out a few dollars and an hour. Best case, you stop being a passenger.
